macro_segments.title: Introduction and Mishnah on Wagon and Mules

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and Mishnah: Selling wagon without mules, selling mules without wagon
Rabbi Yehuda's view: Money indicates intent (hadamim modiyim)
Example case: Price of 200 zuz reveals cattle meant, not yoke alone
Chachamim's view: Money cannot reinterpret word meanings

macro_segments.title: Gemara debate on hadamim modiyim vs. fixed word meanings

micro_segments.title: Braitah from Rav Tachlifa: Selling wagon does sell mules when harnessed together
Rav Yafas resolution: Braitah applies when mules are strapped to wagon
Analysis: Word meaning depends on whether subset of population uses term that way
Rashbam's explanation: burden of proof on buyer; majority population meaning controls
Gemara question: If you overpaid for yoke, shouldn't sale be void (ona'ah)?
First answer: Overpayment voids sale as mekach ta'ut (mistaken transaction)
Second answer: No mistake where nobody thinks yoke worth 10,000; treat as gift
Rashbam analysis: First answer more logical; second answer fits Mishnah better

macro_segments.title: Mishnah on Donkey and Its Equipment

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: Selling donkey does not sell its equipment (kelav)
Nachum HaMadi view: Selling donkey does sell equipment
Rabbi Yehuda view: Sometimes yes, sometimes no—depends on context and word choice
Ulla's clarification: Debate is about sacks, bags, and side saddles (koumni)
Tanna Kamma: Donkey used primarily for riding; sacks for carrying are not included
Nachum HaMadi: Donkey also used for carrying burden; sacks are included

macro_segments.title: Gemara on equipment sold with donkey: When attached or explicitly mentioned

micro_segments.title: Braitah: Saddle, blanket, and straps sold if explicit phrase 'kelav' used
Gemara interpretation: Even without 'kelav' saddle is sold; braitah teaches sacks aren't
Question: Is debate when equipment is on donkey or when it isn't?
Resolution: Debate when equipment not on it; all agree when on it, everything sold
Ravina to Rav Ashi: Evidence from Rabbi Yehuda shows debate includes case when on donkey
Analogy from wagon and mules: When connected, all sold together; when not, debate exists
Conclusion: Each case unique; cargo on ship not sold; items' integral nature varies

macro_segments.title: Tannaim consensus on selling items with equipment and Rabbi Yehuda's contextual approach

micro_segments.title: Amoraim statement: All tannaim hold sale includes all equipment in appropriate contexts
Examples: Rabbi Eliezer (olive press with beam), Rabban Shimon (city with sentry)
Rabbi Meir: Vineyard sold with vineyard equipment (Rashbam notes attribution issue)
Rabbi Natan and Sumchos: Small boats sold with larger ship
Rabbi Yehuda: Context determines if equipment included—'this donkey' vs. 'your donkey'
Rava explanation: 'This one' emphasizes current state with equipment; 'your donkey' does not

macro_segments.title: Mishnah: Animals and refuse included or excluded in sales

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: Donkey sold with baby donkey (sayach); cow not sold with calf
Mishnah: Selling plot of ground (ashpa) includes manure (zivlah)
Mishnah: Cistern sold with water; beehive with bees; pigeon coop with pigeons
Rashbam note: Contents obviously sold; reverse not necessarily true

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Why donkey with baby but cow without calf?

micro_segments.title: Analysis: Depends on whether 'nursing' (menika) is mentioned as descriptor
Rav Pappa: Nursing cow sold because milk is its primary use; nursing donkey not for milk
Conclusion: 'Nursing donkey' implies it comes with baby, not with calf for dairy
Etymology: Sayach (baby donkey) from siach (nice speech), following like seduction

macro_segments.title: Aggadic derasha on Sichon, Moab, and accounting of the world (cheshbono shel olam)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's derasha on 'then shall the poets say' (al kein yomru ha'meshalim)
Meshalim as 'masters of desire'; Cheshbon as calculating one's larger accounting
Tibaneh v'tikonen: Built in this world, established in world to come if you calculate properly
Ir Sichon: Baby donkey (ayir) seduced by nice speech; parallel to one seduced by yetzer hara
Fire from Cheshbon: Righteous whose accounting destroys those led by appetite
Bamot Arnon as arrogant ones; Neiram as 'ein ram' (no God); judgment comes for all

macro_segments.title: Final aggadic teaching on leaving Torah results in destruction by fire and Gehinnom

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda in name of Rav: Whoever leaves Torah words consumed by fire
Rav Dimi in name of Rabbi Yochanan: Whoever leaves Torah falls to Gehinnom
Refaim identified as Gehinnom; those leaving knowledge rest in depths of Sheol

topical_tags.term: cheshbono shel olam (accounting of the world)
context and word choice determining sale
tannaim holding equipment sold with items
